key_departures.impact: Davis was the unquestioned heart of the Saints defense for 8 seasons, leading the team in stops for an eighth consecutive year in 2025 with 143 tackles and 17 starts. His departure removes the team's most important defensive leader and play-caller at linebacker. The Elliss signing partially fills this role but cannot replicate Davis's veteran leadership and captain status.

Taylor was the Saints' best cornerback and one of the top free agent corners in the entire class. He recorded 83 tackles, 11 passes defensed, and 2 interceptions in 2025 with 44 starts over three seasons. New Orleans lost him for a top-of-market deal they were unwilling to match. CB is now a critical need with no clear replacement on the roster.

key_departures.contract_signed: N/A — unrestricted free agent as of March 11
key_departures.impact: Jordan became a free agent for the first time in his 15-year career when his voiding contract expired. He led the Saints with 10.5 sacks in 2025, his most since 2021, and remains a productive starter. His departure (or continued absence) leaves a significant hole opposite Chase Young on the defensive line. As of March 16, Jordan remains unsigned and his return is unresolved.

Fortner stepped in as starting center after Erik McCoy was injured in Week 7 and was a stabilizing presence for the offense down the stretch. His departure to Carolina leaves New Orleans without a reliable backup center behind McCoy, creating a depth concern at IOL.

Stoll was the Saints' primary blocking tight end in 2025. His departure is a minor loss given the team signed Noah Fant, who brings superior receiving ability and comparable blocking. Net effect is essentially neutral — the position upgraded.

Hill's unique utility role (QB/TE/RB hybrid) is irreplaceable and his departure ends a 9-year tenure. At 36 and with retirement possible, this is a modest scheme loss more than a positional need. The Saints may absorb this via internal options.

Kroeger averaged only 37.3 net yards per punt in 2025, ranking among the NFL's worst. The Saints traded him and a 2028 seventh-round pick to Houston for a 2029 sixth-round pick after signing Ryan Wright. A clear upgrade situation.

signings.analysis: Elite signing at the RB position. Etienne rushed for 1,107 yards and 13 total touchdowns in 2025 for Jacksonville and is a premier pass-catching back. The 4yr/$52M deal with $28M guaranteed signals full commitment — this is their lead back for the contract duration. The Saints' rushing attack ranked 28th in 2025; Etienne directly solves that. His arrival effectively ends Alvin Kamara's role as the featured back. At $13M AAV, this is top-of-market for RB. RB need drops from a priority to non-existent.

The top guard available in the 2026 free agent class, signed to the richest deal of the class at 4yr/$61M with $45M guaranteed. Edwards started 16 games for Buffalo in 2025 and was part of the Bills' run-blocking-dominant line. He projects as the immediate starter at left guard, a position the Saints had addressed only via short-term deals for years. $45M in guarantees on a 4-year deal is a fully committed investment — left guard is addressed long-term. The re-signing of Radunz as depth further solidifies the IOL room. IOL need drops significantly, though backup center depth (post-Fortner) remains a minor concern.

Elliss was a 7th-round pick by the Saints in 2019 who developed into a 17-game starter and captain for the Falcons before returning to New Orleans. The 3yr/$33M deal with $23M guaranteed reflects a real multi-year commitment at a starter-level contract. He addresses the void left by Demario Davis signing with the Jets. However, Elliss at 30 is not Davis's peer as a playmaker or leader — Davis posted 143 tackles and multiple Pro Bowl-caliber seasons while Davis is just now stepping into a full-time role. The LB need is partially addressed: a starter is in place, but the room lacks the same elite anchor it had with Davis. LB remains a draft consideration.

Fant is a former first-round pick (No. 20 in 2019) with proven pass-catching ability who joins as the No. 2 behind Juwan Johnson. The 2yr/$8.75M deal is a depth-tier contract but Fant can upgrade into a starter-quality role in 12-personnel. He had 34 catches for 388 yards and 3 TDs with Cincinnati in 2025, plus elite run-blocking grades (82.3% win rate). His arrival makes the departure of Jack Stoll and Taysom Hill less impactful. TE was not a top-tier need entering FA, and with Johnson returning, this signing provides quality depth. The position is now reasonably stocked.

Ridgeway is a depth defensive tackle who was acquired for a Day 3 pick in 2024. He missed significant time in 2025 on injured reserve. The 2yr/$6.2M deal with just $1.65M guaranteed is a low-commitment depth signing. He provides rotation help but doesn't address any starter-level IDL need. Re-signing him maintains continuity but IDL remains a draft priority.

The Saints had a carousel at punter since Thomas Morstead left after 2020, cycling through Blake Gillikin, Lou Hedley, Matthew Hayball, and Kai Kroeger (who averaged a league-worst 37.3 net yards per punt). Wright averaged 49 yards per punt in 2025 for Minnesota — a massive upgrade. The 4yr/$14M deal with $8M guaranteed is a top-5 punter contract and a real long-term commitment. This eliminates special teams as a recurring organizational concern. Wright becomes the 5th highest-paid punter in the NFL at $3.5M AAV.

Radunz started 10-of-15 games at left guard in 2025 but was graded 72nd of 79 qualifying guards by PFF. With Edwards signed as the starter, Radunz slots as experienced interior line depth and a swing guard. Financial terms of the 2-year deal have not been fully disclosed as of March 16. This is a continuity/depth signing — he knows the system and provides insurance. Does not address any positional need independently but complements the Edwards signing. Contract terms flagged as TBD pending official disclosure.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Etienne signing (4yr/$52M, $28M guaranteed) fully solves the position. He was 2nd-best RB on the FA market and addresses the Saints' 28th-ranked rushing attack directly. Alvin Kamara's role is now uncertain/reduced. RB is no longer a draft need.

Edwards (4yr/$61M, $45M guaranteed) was the top guard in the class and brings starting-caliber left guard play. Radunz provides depth. Fortner's departure to Carolina creates a minor backup center concern, but the starter problem is solved. IOL drops to a late-round depth consideration only.

Elliss (3yr/$33M, $23M guaranteed) fills the starter void left by Demario Davis, but doesn't replicate Davis's elite playmaking or leadership. At 30 with a multi-year deal, Elliss is a real investment. The Saints may still want a developmental LB in the mid-rounds of the draft to build depth alongside Elliss long-term.

Taylor's departure to Tennessee (3yr/$60M) leaves a massive hole at CB1. Taylor was the team's best corner and one of the top FAs in the class. No replacement was signed. With no credible starter at CB behind the remaining depth, CB becomes a critical need — the top defensive priority entering the draft at No. 8 overall.

Cameron Jordan (10.5 sacks in 2025) remains unsigned as of March 16 and is actively exploring other teams including the Chiefs. His departure leaves Chase Young without a capable partner on the edge. If Jordan does not return, EDGE becomes a co-top need alongside CB. Even if Jordan returns on a short bridge deal, EDGE remains a priority given his age (36). Flag this position as fluid — tier should be reassessed when Jordan's status is resolved.

Fant adds quality depth behind Juwan Johnson. Taysom Hill and Jack Stoll departed, but the Johnson-Fant pairing is the best Saints TE duo in years. TE drops slightly as a need — it's now depth/developmental, not a starter priority.

Ridgeway re-signing at depth money doesn't change the need picture. IDL remains a mid-round draft target. The interior line still needs a true starter-quality complement to prevent one-dimensional run defense.

No meaningful WR additions were made in free agency. The Saints still lack a proven No. 2 receiver to complement their top targets. This need is unchanged and remains a draft priority — likely rounds 1-3.

key_additions.headline: Louisiana native returns home on elite deal to fix the Saints' 28th-ranked rushing attack
Top guard on the market lands in New Orleans on fully committed deal to anchor offensive line for Tyler Shough
Original Saints 7th-round pick returns from Atlanta as a proven starter to fill the Demario Davis void
Former first-round TE adds receiving and blocking depth to give Saints a legitimate two-TE threat
Tulane alum ends years of punter instability with top-5 contract and 49 ypp average in 2025
Depth DT re-signed to maintain interior rotation continuity
Versatile interior lineman retained as experienced depth behind starter David Edwards

key_losses.headline: 8-year Saints captain and defensive play-caller departs to rejoin the Jets, ending one of the franchise's greatest free agent success stories
Saints' best cornerback lands top-of-market deal in Tennessee, creating the team's most pressing draft need heading into the top-10
Franchise sack leader (132 career, 10.5 in 2025) hits free agency for first time after 15 seasons; return uncertain with Chiefs interest reported
Backup center who stepped up after Erik McCoy's injury leaves for a starting opportunity in Carolina
Blocking tight end departs after one season; made redundant by Noah Fant signing
Struggling punter (37.3 net ypp, bottom of league) traded away after Saints upgraded to Ryan Wright

notes: Cameron Jordan's status is the most significant unresolved variable as of March 16. He is confirmed unsigned and exploring the open market. If he signs elsewhere, EDGE and CB become co-equal Tier 1 draft priorities at No. 8. Alvin Kamara's future with the team also remains uncertain following the Etienne signing — his status could create or remove a depth need at RB. Dillon Radunz contract financial terms have not been publicly disclosed as of the updated_through date; total_value and guaranteed fields set to 0 pending confirmation. Travis Etienne guaranteed money confirmed at $28M per Louisiana Sports/official reporting. David Edwards guaranteed confirmed at $45M per ESPN.
